title-group.article-title: Serious adverse events following human papillomavirus vaccination: A systematic review and meta-analysis of post-marketing evidence

contrib-group.aff: Department of Epidemiology and Biostatistics, School of Public Health, SUNY Downstate Health Sciences University, Brooklyn, NY, USA

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccines are a critical tool in cancer prevention, yet concerns about serious adverse events (SAEs) have fueled public hesitancy and constrained vaccination uptake. This systematic review aimed to determine the proportion of adverse events that were SAEs, rate of SAEs following HPV vaccination and their association from post-marketing observational studies. We conducted a search in PubMed, EMBASE, and Web of Science databases in March 2025. Studies published in English reporting SAEs after HPV vaccination from post-licensure studies or surveillance were included. Of the 258 identified records, 24 articles were included in the systematic review: 15 in the meta-analysis and 9 in the narrative synthesis. Among the studies included in the meta-analysis, four found no SAEs; the pooled proportion of adverse events that were SAEs was 3.67% (95% confidence interval, CI: 1.19%, 7.30%), I2 = 99.73%. The pooled rate of SAEs in 14 studies that reported total number of doses distributed was 0.04 (95% CI: 0.00, 0.35) per 1000 doses, I2 = 99.98%. No consistent evidence of any increased risk of SAEs with HPV vaccination was found in the nine studies included in the narrative synthesis. However, an increased risk of celiac disease was observed, likely due to diagnostic unmasking rather than a causal association, warranting further investigation. Our findings provide evidence that SAEs remain rare following HPV vaccination. Nevertheless, continued surveillance and improved standardization of SAE definition and reporting are essential to strengthen post-marketing safety monitoring and counteract vaccine misinformation.

funding-group.funding-statement: The author(s) reported there is no funding associated with the work featured in this article.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Human papillomavirus (HPV), particularly the genotypes that are causally linked to several cancers such as cervical, penile, vaginal, vulvar, oral, laryngeal, and oropharyngeal cancers, poses a significant global health burden. HPV was attributable to an estimated 831,204 (88.6% in women and 11.4% in men) cases of cancers and 422,935 deaths (89.6% in women and 10.4% in men) globally in 2022.1 In the United States (US), the age-standardized incidence rate of HPV-attributable cancer was 20.3 per 100,000, and the age-standardized mortality rate was 4.5 per 100,000.1 An estimated 37,800 new cases of HPV-attributable cancer cases are diagnosed annually in the US.2

Three vaccines are licensed in the US for prevention of HPV-associated cancers caused by high-risk oncogenic types (16, 18, 31, 33, 35, 39, 45, 51, 56, 58, and 59) and non-cancerous conditions such as anogenital warts caused by low-risk types (notably 6 and 11). The quadrivalent vaccine (4vHPV; Gardasil), approved in 2006, targets HPV types 6, 11, 16, and 18, the bivalent vaccine (2vHPV; Cervarix) was approved in 2009 to protect against high-risk types 16 and 18, and the 9-valent vaccine (9vHPV; Gardasil 9) was approved in 2014 expanding coverage to five additional high-risk types (31, 33, 45, 52, and 58) in addition to the four types included in the 4vHPV. The US Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) recommends routine vaccination for everyone aged 11–12 y, which can begin as early as 9 y of age, with catch-up vaccination through age 26 y, and shared clinical decision-making for adults aged 27–45 y.3 Individuals initiating vaccination before 15 y of age are recommended to receive a two-dose schedule, while those who are immunocompromised or initiating at age 15 y or older are advised to complete a three-dose series.4 Beyond its clinical impact, HPV vaccination is a cost-effective cancer prevention strategy.5–7

Despite the availability of safe and effective vaccines since 2006, vaccination coverage in the US continues to lag behind national targets. As of 2022, only 38.6% of the children aged 9–17 y received one or more doses of the HPV vaccine,8 while only 57.3% of adolescents aged 13–15 y completed the recommended 2- or 3-dose HPV vaccine series. This falls short of the Healthy People 2030 target of 80% vaccination coverage of the recommended doses.9 One major barrier to vaccine uptake is increasing parental vaccine hesitancy driven by safety concerns.10–12 Recommendation from a healthcare provider is a strong motivator for parental intent to vaccinate children,11,13,14 however, healthcare workers’ concerns about HPV vaccine safety were found to be a critical barrier to recommending HPV vaccination.15

Serious adverse event (SAE), a key metric in vaccine safety monitoring, is defined by the US Code of Federal Regulations as “any adverse experience occurring at any dose that results in any of the following outcomes: Death, a life-threatening adverse experience, inpatient hospitalization or prolongation of existing hospitalization, a persistent or significant disability/incapacity, or a congenital anomaly/birth defect.”16 Extensive randomized clinical trials (RCTs) and meta-analyses of data from such trials have consistently demonstrated that HPV vaccines — including 2vHPV, 4vHPV, and 9vHPV formulations — are highly safe, with rates of SAEs comparable to placebo across diverse populations.17–20 Most adverse events associated with HPV vaccination are mild and localized, such as pain, swelling, or redness at the injection site; the risk of systemic reactions and new onset chronic diseases is low and not significantly different from controls.

Although clinical trials consistently demonstrate a favorable safety profile, concerns persist among healthcare providers and the public. While RCTs offer high internal validity, their controlled environments, stringent inclusion criteria, and limited follow-up duration constrain generalizability to broader populations that receive vaccines. As such, they do not adequately capture the real-world, post-licensure safety profile of the HPV vaccine, particularly across diverse populations and healthcare settings. Existing systematic reviews of HPV vaccine safety have primarily synthesized adverse events from pre-licensure RCTs, and no prior review has comprehensively evaluated post-marketing observational evidence on SAE. We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis to address this evidentiary gap by systematically synthesizing evidence from post-marketing surveillance and studies to determine the proportion of adverse events that were SAE and the rates of SAE. The findings will inform public health policy and communication to counter misinformation and strengthen vaccine confidence among healthcare providers and parents to improve HPV vaccine uptake and reduce the global burden of HPV-related cancers and other diseases.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis in accordance with PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) guidelines (Supplementary material).21 No review protocol exists, and the systematic review was not registered.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: A literature search was performed on March 31, 2025, across three electronic databases – PubMed, EMBASE, and Web of Science – from 2006 to search date. Search terms included combinations of keywords, using the Boolean operator AND to identify relevant studies: “human papillomavirus vaccin*” AND “serious” AND “adverse.” Reference lists of eligible articles and relevant reviews were also screened for additional studies. All identified records were imported into online reference management software Covidence for deduplication and screening.22

sec.title: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
sec.p: Only post-marketing observational studies and surveillance published in English from 2006 onward reporting SAEs following HPV vaccination were included. Eligible studies defined SAE and reported the total number of doses distributed or administered, as well as the total number of adverse events and SAEs to allow calculation of the SAE reporting rate. In the narrative synthesis, we also included studies that did not explicitly define SAE, did not report SAE-specific results or the total number of doses distributed, adverse events, or SAEs, and those that combined hospitalized patients with patients seen in emergency and outpatient settings comprising both serious and non-serious adverse events. Randomized controlled trials, pre-licensure studies, animal studies, case reports, and commentaries were excluded to ensure relevance to real-world vaccine safety.

sec.title: Study selection and data extraction
sec.p: Following de-duplication, titles and abstracts were screened for relevance by HB. Then full-text articles of potentially eligible studies were assessed for inclusion separately by both HB and SN. Disagreements were resolved by discussion and consensus. Data were extracted systematically into a standardized template (excel file), capturing the following variables: author, year, country of study, study design, study period, study participants, sample size and characteristics (e.g., age, sex), exposure, outcome, key findings, limitations, and study funding source.

sec.title: Study quality assessment
sec.p: The quality of the observational studies included in meta-analysis was assessed using a modified Joanna Briggs Institute Prevalence Critical Appraisal Tool tailored to the objective and outcome measure of this review.23 We modified the tool to account for reporting of vaccine dose distributed or administered, and SAE definition and ascertainment. Each study was assessed for eight criteria: representativeness of sample, participant recruitment, sample size, description of participants and setting, response rate, report of dose distributed or administered, SAE definition, and SAE ascertainment. Higher scores reflected greater methodological quality and lower risk of bias.

sec.title: Data analysis
sec.p: We first summarized the study characteristics of all studies included in this systematic review. For the studies that defined the SAEs and reported the number of doses distributed, adverse events, and SAEs, we calculated two measures: (1) the proportion of adverse events classified as SAEs, obtained by dividing the number of SAEs by the total number of adverse events, and (2) the reporting rate of SAEs, calculated as the number of SAEs divided by the total number of doses distributed or administered, expressed per 1000 doses. These estimates were pooled across studies using random-effects models using the restricted maximum likelihood (REML) estimator to account for between-study heterogeneity; pooling of reporting rates was limited to studies that reported the total number of doses distributed, including studies reporting no SAEs. Heterogeneity between studies was assessed using the I2 statistic, with an I2 score of >75% indicating high heterogeneity. Subgroup analysis and meta-regression were conducted to investigate potential causes of heterogeneity. Subgroup analysis was conducted by HPV vaccine type (2vHPV, 4vHPV or 9vHPV) and study size (larger studies versus smaller studies). We considered studies with more than 50,000 doses distributed or administered as larger studies, and studies with fewer than 50,000 doses distributed or administered as smaller studies. We conducted random-effects meta-regression to examine study-level moderators such as the publication year (centered around the mean) and study region (North America [USA and Canada] as the reference category, Europe [Italy, Slovenia], and Asia [South Korea, Cambodia and China]) simultaneously. We also conducted two sensitivity analyses based on study quality assessment. The first sensitivity analysis included studies with high SAE ascertainment excluding studies with intermediate or low SAE ascertainment, and the second sensitivity analysis included studies that met seven or more quality indicators. We conducted subgroup analysis and meta-regression for the studies included in each sensitivity analysis. Meta-analyses and meta-regression were conducted in STATA 19.5 (StataCorp LP, College Station, TX). For studies that did not define SAEs, present SAE-specific results, report total number of doses distributed/adverse events/SAEs, or that presented adverse events from hospitalized patients together with those from other settings, we summarized the key findings narratively rather than included in the quantitative analyses.

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
The initial search yielded 258 records, and an additional 12 were included from citation searching. After removing duplicates and screening titles and abstracts, full-text available from 95 studies were assessed. A total of 24 studies were included (Figure 1); 15 studies were included in the meta-analysis24–38 and 9 studies were summarized narratively.39–47 Figure 1.
PRISMA flow diagram for systematic review article inclusion.

sec.title: Characteristics of the included studies
sec.p:
The studies spanned a publication period from 2008 to 2023 and varied widely in terms of the number of distributed doses (Table 1). Most studies were conducted in high-income countries, with the largest representation from the US (n = 8),24,30–32,34,35,44,46 followed by Denmark and Sweden (n = 3),41–43 South Korea (n = 3),28,38,45 Italy (n = 3),25,29,36 China (n = 2),37,47 and single studies from Canada,26 The Netherlands,40 Slovenia,27 and the United Kingdom;39 only one study was from a lower middle-income country (Cambodia).33 HPV vaccines evaluated included the 2vHPV alone (n = 8),25,28,30,33,37–40 4vHPV alone (n = 9),24,26,27,31,34,41–44 and 9vHPV alone (n = 4),32,36,46,47 as well as both 2vHPV and 4vHPV (n = 2),29,45 and combined 4vHPV and 9vHPV (n = 1) 35 vaccines. Study designs and data sources varied and included active surveillance (n = 2),44,46 passive surveillance (n = 9),24,26,27,29–32,35,36 active and enhanced passive surveillance (n = 1),37 catch-up campaign with active follow-up (n = 1),40 post-marketing surveillance with active follow-up (n = 3),28,38,47 national health administrative data (n = 4),41–43,45 prospective cohort study (n = 3),25,34,39 and survey (n = 1).33 Studies using data mostly from passive surveillance with a large number of distributed doses generally identified fewer adverse events and SAEs, while studies with active follow-up and relatively smaller number of doses administered reported higher number of adverse events and SAEs. Study participants were mostly girls and women between 9 and 45 y of age, although nine studies also included male participants.Table 1.
Characteristics of the studies included in this review.

sec.title: Serious adverse events following HPV vaccination in studies included in the meta-analysis
Proportion of adverse events that were serious
sec.p:
All 15 studies in the meta-analysis defined SAE and reported the number of total adverse events and SAEs, which were included to estimate the pooled proportion of adverse events that were classified as serious. The total number of doses distributed or administered was reported in 14 studies, which was 113,839,996 HPV vaccine doses. A total of 72,832 adverse events and 2,929 SAEs were reported following HPV vaccination across the 15 studies; 4 studies found no SAEs.25,33,34,38 The proportion of adverse events that were serious varied from as low as 0.35% to as high as 22.73% in the remaining 11 studies. The pooled proportion of adverse events that were SAEs across the 15 studies was 3.67% (95% confidence interval, CI: 1.19%, 7.30%), I2 = 99.73% (Figure 2). Figure 2.
Forest plot showing study-specific and pooled estimates of the proportion of adverse events (AE) that were serious adverse events (SAEs) following HPV vaccination.

In subgroup analysis by HPV vaccine type, the pooled proportion of adverse events that were serious was 1.64% (95% CI: 0.00%, 7.11%), I2 = 98.71% for 2vHPV, 3.41% (95% CI: 0.73%, 7.83%), I2 = 99.51% for 4vHPV, and 9.01% (95% CI: 0.00%, 37.41%), I2 = 90.87% for 9vHPV vaccine (Figure 3A). In subgroup analysis according to study size, the pooled proportion of adverse events that were serious was 6.09% (95% CI: 3.35%, 9.52%), I2 = 99.39% for studies with a large number of doses distributed, and 0.87% (95% CI: 0.00%, 5.54%), I2 = 99.17% for studies with a smaller number of doses distributed (Figure 3B). Figure 3.
Forest plots showing study-specific and pooled estimates of the proportion of adverse events (AE) that were serious adverse events (SAEs) following HPV vaccination by (A) HPV vaccine type, and (B) study size.

In meta-regression analysis, neither publication year nor study region was associated with the proportion of adverse events that were serious (Wald χ2 = 1.88, p = .5968). The model did not explain between-study heterogeneity (R2 = 0%), and substantial residual heterogeneity remained (I2 = 99.63%) (Table 2).Table 2.
Meta-regression results.

sec.title: Reporting rate of serious adverse event
sec.p:
To estimate the pooled SAE reporting rate, we included 14 studies that reported the total number of doses distributed, including the four studies with zero SAEs. The reporting rate of SAEs ranged from as low as 0.00 per 1000 doses in studies that reported zero SAEs to as high as 4.53 per 1000 doses.37 The overall reporting rate of SAEs from these 14 studies was 0.04 (95% CI: 0.00, 0.35) per 1000 doses, I2 = 99.98% (Figure 4). Figure 4.
Forest plot showing study-specific and pooled estimates of reporting rates of serious adverse events (SAEs) per 1000 doses of HPV vaccines in studies that reported the number of doses distributed.

In subgroup analysis by HPV vaccine type, the pooled reporting rate of SAE was 0.31 (95% CI: 0.00, 1.72) per 1000 doses, I2 = 95.92% for 2vHPV, 0.00 (95% CI: 0.00, 0.00) per 1000 doses, I2 = 98.39% for 4vHPV, and 1.01 (95% CI: 0.00, 0.03) per 1000 doses, I2 = 75.26% for 9vHPV (Figure 5A). In subgroup analysis according to study size, the pooled reporting rate of SAE was 0.02 (95% CI: 0.01, 0.03) per 1000 doses, I2 = 98.57% for the eight studies with large number of doses distributed, and 0.38 (95% CI: 0.00, 1.85) per 1000 doses, I2 = 89.12% for the six studies with smaller number of doses distributed, including four studies with zero SAE (Figure 5B). Figure 5.
Forest plots showing study-specific and pooled estimates of reporting rates of serious adverse events (SAEs) per 1000 doses of HPV vaccines by (A) HPV vaccine type, and (B) study size in studies that included the number of doses distributed.

In meta-regression analysis, the overall model significantly explained between-study heterogeneity (R2 = 69.6%, Wald χ2 = 22.6, p < .0001) although substantial residual heterogeneity remained (I2 = 99.93%) (Table 2). There was no statistically significant association with publication year or the study being conducted in Europe. There was a statistically significant association with studies being conducted in Asia versus North America.

sec.title: Quality of the studies included in meta-analysis
sec.p:
The methodological rigor of the studies included in meta-analysis ranged from low to high, largely depending on sample representativeness, response rate, and SAE ascertainment (Table 3). Of the 15 studies included in the meta-analysis, 13 studies met 6 or more criteria. One study did not include a representative sample and did not report the response rate thus meeting five criteria,38 while another study did not have a representative sample, participant recruitment was unclear, they did not provide response rate, and SAE ascertainment was low with self-report scoring 4.28 SAE ascertainment was low for six studies,25,28,33,34,37,38 intermediate for one study,27 and high for the remaining studies.24,26,29–32,35,36Table 3.

aNon-probability sampling was considered as not representative.
bParticipant recruitment for studies using national surveillance were considered adequate.
cA sample size was considered as adequate if the study included national surveillance or health administrative data, or included most of those eligible, otherwise considered as inadequate.
dA response rate of <50% was considered as low = 0, between 50–80% was considered as intermediate = 0.5 and >80% was considered as high = 1; for studies using health administrative data for which a response rate was not applicable, we assigned high = 1 score; studies using data from passive surveillance while likely suffer from underreporting of mild adverse events, it is unlikely that SAE were underreported and so was considered as high = 1.
eSAE ascertainment: self-report was considered low = 0, use of ICD-9/ICD-10/MedDRA codes without confirmation by record review was considered as intermediate = 0.5, and use of ICD-9/ICD-10/MedDRA codes with confirmation by record review was considered as high = 1.

sec.title: Sensitivity analysis including studies with high SAE ascertainment
sec.p: For proportion of adverse events that were SAEs, eight studies were included that had high SAE ascertainment. The proportion varied from 2.57% to 22.73% with a pooled proportion of 6.71% (95% CI: 3.62%, 10.60%), I2 = 99.49% (Appendix 1, Figure S1). In subgroup analysis by HPV vaccine type, the pooled proportion was 5.88% (95% CI: 5.44%, 6.33%), I2 = 48.10% for 4vHPV and 9.01% (95% CI: 0.00%, 37.41%), I2 = 90.87% for 9vHPV (Appendix 1, Figure S2). Subgroup analysis by study size was not done as all studies were large. In meta-regression analysis, the overall model significantly explained between-study heterogeneity (R2 = 85.8%, Wald χ2 = 21.41, p < .0001) although substantial residual heterogeneity remained (I2 = 96.27%) (Appendix 1, Table S1). There was no statistically significant association with publication year. There was a statistically significant association with the studies being conducted in Europe versus North America.

For the reporting rates of SAEs, seven studies were included that had high SAE ascertainment. The pooled reporting rate of SAEs was 0.02 (95% CI: 0.01, 0.03) per 1000 doses, I2 = 98.44% (Appendix 1, Figure S3). In subgroup analysis by HPV vaccine type, the pooled reporting rate of SAEs was 0.02 (95% CI: 0.01, 0.03) per 1000 doses, I2 = 98.41% for 4vHPV and 0.01 (95% CI: 0.00, 0.03) per 1000 doses, I2 = 75.26% for 9vHPV (Appendix 1, Figure S4). Subgroup analysis by study size was not done as all studies were large. In meta-regression analysis, the overall model significantly explained between-study heterogeneity (R2 = 64.86%, Wald χ2 = 10.95, p = .0042) although substantial residual heterogeneity remained (I2 = 95.23%) (Appendix 1, Table S1). There was a statistically significant association with publication year and with the study being conducted in Europe.

sec.title: Sensitivity analysis including studies that met 7 or more quality indicators
sec.p: For the proportion of adverse events that were SAEs, 11 studies met 7 or more quality indicators. The proportion varied from 0% to 22.73% with a pooled proportion of 4.49% (95% CI: 1.69%, 8.39%), I2 = 99.71% (Appendix 2, Figure S1). In subgroup analysis by HPV vaccine type, the pooled proportion was 0.54% (95% CI: 0.00%, 4.46%), I2 = 93.01% for 2vHPV, 5.77% (95% CI: 5.28%, 6.28%), I2 = 47.84% for 4vHPV and 9.01% (95% CI: 0.00%, 37.41%), I2 = 90.87% for 9vHPV (Appendix 2, Figure S2A). In subgroup analysis by study type, the pooled proportion was 6.09% (95% CI: 3.35%, 9.52%), I2 = 99.39% for larger studies and 0.00% (95% CI: 0.00%, 0.00%), I2 = 0.00% for smaller studies (Appendix 2, Figure S2B). In meta-regression analysis, neither publication year nor study region was associated with the proportion of adverse events that were serious (Wald χ2 = 2.93, p = .4029). The model did not explain between-study heterogeneity (R2 = 0%), and substantial residual heterogeneity remained (I2 = 99.65%) (Appendix 2, Table S2).

For the reporting rates of SAE, 10 studies met at least 7 quality indicators. The pooled reporting rate of SAE was 0.00 (95% CI: 0.00, 0.00) per 1000 doses, I2 = 98.15% (Appendix 2, Figure S3). In subgroup analysis by HPV vaccine type, the pooled reporting rate of SAE was 0.00 (95% CI: 0.00, 0.00) per 1000 doses, I2 = 0.00% for 2vHPV, and 0.02 (95% CI: 0.01, 0.04) per 1000 doses, I2 = 98.78% for 4vHPV, and 0.01 (95% CI: 0.00, 0.03) per 1000 doses, I2 = 75.26% for 9vHPV (Appendix 2, Figure S4). In subgroup analysis by study size, the pooled reporting rate was 0.02 (95% CI: 0.01, 0.03) per 1000 doses, I2 = 98.57% for larger studies, and 0.00 (95% CI: 0.00, 0.02) per 1000 doses, I2 = 0.01% for smaller studies. In meta-regression analysis, the overall model significantly explained between-study heterogeneity (R2 = 71.29%, Wald χ2 = 14.91, p = .0019) although substantial residual heterogeneity remained (I2 = 93.22%) (Appendix 2, Table S1). There was a statistically significant association with publication year and with the study being conducted in Europe.

sec.title: Serious adverse events following HPV vaccines from studies included in the narrative synthesis
sec.p: Nine studies were included in the narrative summary; these studies either did not define SAE, did not report the total number of adverse events, or included patients from inpatient, outpatient and emergency department visits that likely represented a mix of serious and nonserious adverse events (Table 1).

sec.title: Studies reporting no serious adverse events following HPV vaccination
sec.p: Among the included studies, two reported that no SAEs occurred following 2vHPV vaccination. These included a school-based prospective study conducted in Greater Manchester, United Kingdom,39 and a prospective study among adolescent girls who participated in a catch-up vaccination program in the Netherlands.40

sec.title: Autoimmune serious adverse events following HPV vaccination
sec.p: Autoimmune outcomes were evaluated primarily in large population-based cohort studies conducted in Denmark and Sweden that assessed the risk of pre-specified adverse events following 4vHPV vaccination.41,42 These studies did not define SAE or specifically report SAEs but included cases from outpatient, inpatient, or emergency department encounters. One of these studies conducted among adolescent girls did not find any evidence of an increased risk of 53 incident hospital-diagnosed autoimmune, neurological and venous thromboembolic events following 4vHPV vaccination; although initial associations were noted for Bechet’s syndrome, Raynaud’s disease and type 1 diabetes, these associations were weak and not temporally related to vaccination on further assessment.41 In contrast, another study examining the association between 4vHPV vaccination and 45 pre-specified serious chronic diseases reported a statistically significant increased risk of celiac disease in both a cohort analysis (incidence rate ratio 1.56 [95% CI: 1.29, 1.89]) and a self-controlled case series analysis (1.65 [95% CI: 1.20, 2.27]); however, the authors have suggested that later unmasking of preexisting celiac disease could potentially explain the observed increased risk.42

sec.title: Neurological serious adverse events following HPV vaccination
sec.p: Neurological outcomes were evaluated across several studies using cohort, self-controlled case series, and self-controlled risk interval designs. In a cohort study conducted among adolescent girls and women, no increased risk of multiple sclerosis or other demyelinating diseases was observed following 4vHPV vaccination employing both cohort (adjusted rate ratio, 0.90 [95% CI: 0.70, 1.15] and 1.00 [95% CI: 0.80, 1.26], respectively) and self-controlled case series analysis (incidence ratio 1.05 [95% CI: 0.79, 1.38] and 1.14 [95% CI: 0.88, 1.47], respectively).43

Another study conducted in the US among adolescent girls and young women evaluated seven pre-specified outcomes and did not identify a statistically significant increased risk following 4vHPV vaccination; outcomes assessed included Guillan–Barré Syndrome (GBS), stroke, venous thromboembolism, appendicitis, seizures, syncope, allergic reactions, and anaphylaxis in cases identified from inpatient, outpatient and emergency department visits.44 Similarly, a study conducted in South Korea among adolescent girls did not identify an association between 2vHPV or 4vHPV vaccination and 33 pre-specified SAEs in either cohort or self-controlled risk interval analyses; however, an increased risk of migraine was observed in the cohort analyses (adjusted relative risk 1.11 [95% CI: 1.02, 1.22]), which was not observed in the self-controlled risk interval design (adjusted relative risk 0.67 [95% CI: 0.58, 0.78]).45

In a study conducted in the US among adolescents and young adults of both sexes, no statistically significant increased risk was observed for GBS, chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP), or stroke in inpatient, outpatient and emergency department visits following 9vHPV vaccination with reporting rates of 0.3 per 100,000 doses for GBS, 0.1 per 100,000 doses for CIDP, and 1.2 per 100,000 doses for stroke.46

sec.title: Other serious adverse events following HPV vaccination
sec.p: One confirmed 4vHPV vaccination-related anaphylaxis with a reporting rate of 1.7 per million doses (95% CI: 0.04, 9.3) was reported in a study conducted in the US.44 Lastly, a study conducted in China assessed the safety of 9vHPV among individuals aged 16–26 y reported 8 SAEs during the study period none of which were found to be related to 9vHPV; however, this study did not define SAE.47

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: We aimed to review existing literature on the safety of HPV vaccines, specifically, SAEs from post-marketing studies. 24 articles were included in this systematic review, 15 included in the meta-analysis, and 9 included in the narrative synthesis. Our findings suggest the proportion of adverse events that were serious and the rate of SAE reporting remain low. There was no consistent evidence of an increased risk of serious adverse events following HPV vaccination. The findings from this review can be used to inform evidence-based public messaging to counter vaccine hesitancy involving vaccine safety misconceptions.

The overall proportion of adverse events that were SAE across the 15 studies was 3.7% in our study with high heterogeneity between the included studies, which was not explained in the subgroup analyses by HPV vaccine type and study size. Meta-regression analysis examining the role of publication year and region where the study was conducted also did not explain the heterogeneity. The overall rate of SAE reporting across the 14 studies that reported the total number of doses distributed was very low at 0.04 per 1000 doses with high heterogeneity between the studies. The heterogeneity decreased considerably to I2 = 75% for 9vHPV vaccine in subgroup analysis by HPV vaccine type and decreased slightly to I2 = 89% for studies with a smaller number of doses distributed. Approximately 70% of the between-study heterogeneity was explained in meta-regression analysis by publication year and study location, with statistically significant association between the studies conducted in Asia being smaller studies.

Our findings should be interpreted in the context of the methodological quality of the included studies. Although most studies met several quality criteria, there was meaningful variation in sample representativeness, response rate reporting, and particularly in the rigor of SAE ascertainment. Studies with lower quality scores more often relied on self-reported SAEs or lacked confirmatory diagnostic record review, whereas higher-quality studies typically used population-based data sources and validated diagnostic coding with medical record confirmation. These methodological differences are likely to have contributed to the substantial heterogeneity observed across studies and underscore the importance of considering study quality when interpreting pooled SAE estimates. In the two sensitivity analyses, we conducted based on the study quality to explore this, high SAE ascertainment and meeting seven or more quality indicators, substantial heterogeneity remained for the pooled estimates of both the proportion of adverse events that were SAEs and the reporting rate of SAEs. Subgroup analysis by HPV vaccine type and/or study size partially explained between-study heterogeneity. Subgroup analysis by HPV vaccine type decreased heterogeneity substantially to I2 = 48.10% for 4vHPV for proportion of adverse events that were SAEs and decreased to I2 = 75.26% for 9vHPV for SAE reporting rate among studies with high SAE ascertainment. In studies that met seven or more quality indicators, heterogeneity decreased to I2 = 47.84% for 4vHPV and heterogeneity eliminated for smaller studies for the proportion of adverse events that were SAEs; for the reporting rates of SAE heterogeneity eliminated for 2vHPV and decreased to I2 = 75.26% for 9vHPV and eliminated for smaller studies. While the overall meta-regression model examining publication year and study region explained between-study heterogeneity to a variable extent, substantial residual heterogeneity persisted. This suggests that there are additional factors beyond those investigated in subgroup and meta-regression analyses even in high-quality studies that have likely contributed to the residual high heterogeneity. Differences in surveillance systems, as well as SAE definitions and ascertainment, reporting of doses distributed compared to doses administered, as well as the international differences in healthcare-seeking behavior and country-specific reporting and surveillance systems all plausibly contributed to the unexplained residual heterogeneity and should be examined in future studies. Additionally, an increase in adverse event reporting immediately after licensure of a new product (known as the “Weber effect”) or following periods of heightened media attention on vaccine safety, may artificially inflate the number of reported events.26,48 Of note, not all reported SAE in the included studies were found to be causally associated with HPV vaccination in causality assessment.24,28,29 As such, high heterogeneity likely reflects methodological diversity of the included post-marketing vaccine safety studies rather than conflicting safety signals.

Nevertheless, our findings align with prior evidence on HPV vaccine safety from clinical trials, which also concluded that HPV vaccines have a favorable safety profile.17,18 In a systematic review of 11 randomized clinical trials comparing the safety of the HPV vaccine with placebo, no vaccine-related SAEs or deaths were reported in 10 studies; only 1 study among 3026 females aged 18–25 y in China reported one case of gastrointestinal infection as a vaccine-related SAE.18 Another systematic review of the safety of vaccines used in routine immunization in the US also did not identify any evidence of increased risk or sufficient evidence of SAEs following 9vHPV vaccination.17 By focusing exclusively on post-licensure observational studies, this review provides more generalizable evidence of real-world vaccine safety across diverse populations and healthcare systems, addressing the limitations of RCTs, such as restricted follow-up periods and homogenous samples.

Within the narrative synthesis, one observational study reported a statistically significant association between 4vHPV vaccination and subsequent diagnosis of celiac disease using both cohort and self-controlled case series analyses.42 However, the authors noted that the observed association may reflect an increased clinical detection or unmasking of preexisting disease rather than a causal vaccine effect. Importantly, this finding was not corroborated by the broader post-marketing evidence synthesized in this review, nor by contemporary observational analyses. For example, a recent propensity score – matched cohort study using US electronic health record data reported reduced risks of several immune-mediated diseases, including celiac disease, among HPV-vaccinated individuals compared with unvaccinated controls.49 As such, current evidence does not suggest a causal association between HPV vaccination and immune-mediated diseases.

sec.title: Strengths and limitations
sec.p: The primary strength of this systematic review is its exclusive focus on post-marketing observational and surveillance data, enabling assessment of SAEs following HPV vaccination under real-world conditions that are not captured in RCTs. Additionally, this systematic review includes a comprehensive search of multiple databases including most recent published articles that also captured studies on the 9vHPV vaccine summarizing evidence from post-marketing vaccine administration in the target population. Unlike previous systematic reviews that focused on adverse events reported in RCTs conducted prior to licensure and unable to capture the safety profile of HPV vaccines as implemented at scale in routine clinical practice, this review fills a critical gap by evaluating real-world SAE reporting across post-licensure settings, thereby extending the evidence base beyond trial populations and enhancing the applicability of safety findings to clinical and public health decision-making.

Despite rigorous methodology, this review has limitations. This study lacked a predefined protocol for meta-analysis and exploration of between study heterogeneity. Analysis decisions on meta-analysis of proportion of adverse events that were SAEs and reporting rate of SAEs were made post-hoc after data extraction based on data availability and feasibility. Subgroup analyses by HPV vaccine type and study size were conducted to assess the observed heterogeneity among the studies in meta-analyses. We also conducted meta-regression and sensitivity analyses based on study quality assessment to further explain heterogeneity between studies. We summarized the key findings from studies that did not meet the criteria to be included in the meta-analysis. Several studies included in this review and meta-analysis used data from national passive surveillance that likely suffered from underreporting mild adverse events; however, it is unlikely that SAEs are underreported in passive surveillance. Indeed, a study in Australia reported similar SAE reporting rates in active and passive surveillance.50 The heterogeneity in study designs and data sources, including absent or inconsistent SAE definition, ascertainment, and reporting precluded us from including all 24 studies in the meta-analysis; this highlights the challenges in data harmonization for meta-analysis and comparative interpretation. This also limited our ability to estimate pooled SAE-specific reporting rates. Use of a standard SAE definition, comparable study design, and data completeness is warranted to improve the strength of evidence generated from post-licensure vaccine safety evaluations. We restricted our search to studies published in English language, which may have excluded studies published in languages other than English. Most of the studies were conducted in high-income countries that limit the generalizability of the findings to other settings. We did not examine publication bias using a funnel plot as it is not considered an appropriate tool for assessing publication bias in presence of substantial heterogeneity and therefore was not suitable in our study context of high heterogeneity between studies.51

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Although subject to limitations, the findings from this systematic review indicate a strong and consistent safety profile for HPV vaccination across a variety of surveillance and study designs. The evidence suggests that SAEs following HPV vaccination are exceedingly rare and not significantly elevated compared to background incidence in the general population. Future research should prioritize ongoing active surveillance efforts in post-licensure populations to ensure the continued monitoring of vaccine safety in real-world settings. It is imperative that future studies incorporate diverse populations, particularly from low- and middle-income countries, where post-marketing data remain limited, and health system contexts may influence the manifestation and reporting of adverse events. Additionally, there is a critical need for the adoption of standardized definitions and reporting criteria for SAEs to facilitate cross-study comparability and meta-analytic synthesis. Longitudinal cohort studies examining long-term outcomes following HPV vaccination would provide valuable insights into the temporal dynamics of vaccine-related risks and further strengthen the evidence base informing public health policy and vaccine confidence.

bio.p: Hannah Brown is a medical student at Sidney Kimmel Medical College at Thomas Jefferson University. She holds an MPH in Epidemiology from the State University of New York (SUNY) Downstate Health Sciences University School of Public Health.

Sharifa Nasreen is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Epidemiology and Biostatistics at the State University of New York (SUNY) Downstate Health Sciences University School of Public Health. She is a medical epidemiologist and holds a PhD in Epidemiology and Biostatistics. Her research focuses on epidemiology of infectious diseases, including vaccine-preventable diseases.
